speaker: interviewer
text: --and a real game. Who-- who are you guys playing? We play like the E channel, Amblin-- we're gonna play Amblin this year. So we play all different-- any kind of studios we play. Oh. So it's fun, you know? And when Michael told me that yesterday, you know, I talked to this guy Friday. I'm kind of hung up right now. I don't know what to-- I mean, I want to stay with Shoah if I can, but if I go-- you know, I've got to go with-- I've got to do something. I would love to stay with Shoah and work, because I enjoy this. But there's not enough-- or, what's the-- what's preventing it? The-- see, this one guy, the Stan guy got married. And he's on his honeymoon. And they want me to fill in for him for three weeks. Oh, I see. And he said I fill in for him for three weeks, and then in June, when they hire more people, they'll hire me, right? And-- Go ahead. Today's date is 24, April 1995. The survivor's name is Leon Wendel. The interviewer is Ellen Baskin. The city is Los Angeles, California. We're in America. Today's language will be in English. Thank you. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Ellen Baskin. It's B-A-S-K-I-N. Today is April 24, 1995. I'm interviewing Leon Wendel, and we're in Los Angeles, California in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to begin by asking you to tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Leon Wendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-L-O-N W-E-N-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's L-E-O-N right?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born Lajwa Wendel, which is L-A-J-W-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: --born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in April 4, 19-- April 7-- my correction-- April 4-- April 7, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a little nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were five brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your parents' names and your brothers' and sister's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Kalman Wendel. And my mother was Esther Hannah Wendel. My oldest brother was Leibel. The next brother was Hershel. Next was my sister Alta or Adela in Polish, or-- I don't know how it would be in English. Then was my brother Manny, or [NON-ENGLISH], my brother Mendel, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest-- the baby to the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the community life like in Lodz at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we lived in a mostly Jewish neighborhood. The city was close to a million people. 1/3 of the population, at least 1/3 of the population, was Jewish. The other 2/3 were about [NON-ENGLISH], or a little more Poles and Germans, or what they called the volksdeutsche, which means native Germans. The-- I had a very happy childhood. I come from a middle class, or what we would call here above middle class family. We lived in a, I would say, 90% Jewish neighborhood. It was not a wealthy neighborhood. It was rather a poor neighborhood. It was called the section of Balut, in the city of Lodz. It's was the old town. And it was a working neighborhood. Mine-- my childhood was a very happy one. I was the baby in the family. And I was-- I wasn't spoiled, but I was very good taken care of. The earliest I can remember is maybe going back to six years or something like that. I don't remember much before that. But I remember distinctly the first day going to school, you know. I-- I was seven years by then, or pretty near seven years to start school. And I went to a public school, which was 100% Jewish. It was run by the government. It was a government school, but it still was a Jewish school, because the neighborhood was Jewish. There were no Christian children in school. We went to school six days a week, included Sunday. We had off on Saturday-- and also very happy years. I finished school. I finished school in-- after seven years. That was-- after that, either you went-- you continued on privately. Or very few people out of the whole school, if I know, if I remember right-- two children got a scholarship to go what they called the-- a gymnasium, which here would be a high school, or a little better than high school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now going back to my childhood, I-- I had lots of friends. We were religious, but not too religious. We observed all the holidays. Saturday was Saturday. But we were not that-- well, maybe my father-- but the children-- we did go to a movie. We would take the streetcar to get out of town in summer to go to-- to a lake or something like that. So we had-- we were not-- we were considered already advanced in-- in religion, or maybe-- I wouldn't say religion. Well, religion was religion. Except, we would do certain things which the real orthodox or ultra orthodox would frown at, you know. They would never do that. We owned a-- a big building in partnership with-- with a uncle and another person. And we were eight in the family included. Every day, to dinner, the whole family was there, eight of us. And my oldest brother was independent. He had, well, I should-- I should go back first to my father. My father was what you would call here a mechanic, or a general mechanic. We had a little shop in the same building we lived in. And he would fix things. In other words, he would fix general things. Somebody needed a lock fixed, he would fix it. Somebody locked himself out, they would call him. He will open it. If a gear was broken-- and he did a lot of that-- a gear was broken in-- in a printing press, and the machinery was from Germany, or from the United States, or from England, and they had to wait maybe two or three weeks until a gear had to be shipped in, he would take that gear home into his shop and add a toot to it, or two or three if necessary. Always he would drill the holes, put in like a bolt, file it nice, then solder it with brass, and make the machine work again, instead of waiting two or three weeks. It wasn't like here you could go out and get a replacement right away or get it by mail overnight. It didn't work like that. Not even the mail worked like that. There was no mail overnight. And besides that, there was income from the house. My oldest brother was a furniture maker. He was, before the war, oh, maybe-- as long as I can remember, he worked for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the baby, and to me, he was already like a grown man. He was-- how many years older than me? Maybe-- we were separated between about a year and a 1/2 each child, or 2 years. So he had to be at least 10 years or a little more older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older-- my oldest brother, Leibel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a furniture maker. And he had a-- he had his own shop. He employed about three, maybe four people sometimes, if necessary. And it was all done by hand. And it was very expensive furniture. I'll give an example. To make a dining set would take them at least two weeks or more. So certain parts he had done by machine. He took it out to a machine shop that specialized in those things. And they would rout it, or you know, like make fancy legs, or the fancy edgings around. And the rest he'd do in the shop. It was all done by hand with hot glue and no nails. It was all dovetailed and very expensive. He was very good at it. And he was doing very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he was-- they wanted to take him to the army. So being the oldest brother, he applied for a referral. Whatever it-- not a referral, a deferral. How would you call it? An-- A deferment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they gave him another year. But it didn't-- he had to go to the army. He was two years in the army, and-- and he was my oldest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My next brother, Hershel, the younger than him, couldn't be younger more than 2 years or a year and a 1/2. He worked for my father, with my father, in my father's shop. And he had-- he-- he-- how would you say that? He was going around with mine-- with a cousin of his, of mine cousin from my mother's side, Sarah. I will come back to that a little later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then my youngest, the next one, was my sister, Alta, or in Polish she called herself Adela. And she was at home. She was a young girl. I mean before the war, she couldn't have been more than 19. Then was my brother, Manny. And he also worked with my father-- with my father. And he survived. And he's around. My other brother, Mendel, finished school maybe a year or two before me-- maybe not even a year or two. And he went to work as a weaver, which consisted in a-- it was home work-- not at our home, but at-- it was somebody had let's say two, three weaving machines. It was done by hand. And he went there in order to learn as an apprentice. And when the war broke out, he was already making about, oh, maybe 15, 20 zlotys a week, which was considered good money. Even a much older working man wasn't making more than that. And the idea was that to set him up into this business, buy a few machines, work a little himself, and then gradually have people work for him, which was very common in the city of Lodz. And I was the youngest, and I finished school in 1938. And the-- the neighborhood we lived in, or the whole neighborhood, all of Balut, was a weaving neighborhood. The-- most people were weavers. It was-- this was the-- the income people had.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took home work from the big factories, and they done it at home at the-- at-- piece for it. In other words, they paid that much for each meter, which is a yard. So they were doing those heavy clothes. How would I say this? What they used for-- you know, when-- when it was winter cold, the woman went out, and she did-- she didn't put on a coat. She took that-- it was like a-- A cape?

speaker: interviewee
text: If wasn't even a cape. It was a square thing with a nice fringe, and she wrapped herself around. It was real warm-- real good.

speaker: interviewer
text: A shawl?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was big. It was not shawl. In summertime, it was shawl. They did that-- they did that too in summertime. And then some made what they called [NON-ENGLISH], which was real beautiful cloth or shawls. They were also big like-- like the Spanish wear in Spain, the Spanish women, those fancy shawls with lots of color. And they also made [NON-ENGLISH] to hang on walls with pictures and thing. It was all done at home. And people lived in the same apartment when this was done. Our house was a big house. It was a four story building. It was a building, an apartment building. And it was like a square. It was very deep in and it-- it-- it was all brick. There were no trees. There was no grass. There was nothing. It was all dry. There was no-- there was no conveniences like running water or running toilets. But we didn't-- we didn't miss any of that, because we didn't know any of that. We knew that in the new city they already have-- we called it-- what would you call it-- plumbing and all the conveniences. And this was the poorer section of town. And I had many friends. In a-- in a house like ours lived there a few hundred people. There-- there must have been a hundred kids in the house or more. There's four stories. There were three buildings with four stories. Two were four stories, and one was three stories. And there's-- and-- in-- in the front house, fronting the street, had that big gate. It's like in a church were you close two halves, and then there's a little gate inside the gate which was closed at night. And you had to ring that big bell to get-- like in a church-- to get the janitor to come and let you in. He had that big key in a ring. You had to give him a tip for that after you woke him up. After 11 o'clock all the houses were closed. It wasn't that you-- there-- it was no curfew or anything like that. But that's the way it was. It was closed. The city got dead after 11 o'clock, except maybe in the weekend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I went to-- to the public school a 1/2 a day till 12 o'clock. I lived on [NON-ENGLISH], which means-- the-- a-- can you cut it a second? What-- what is--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- you know, the-- the-- the navy. How would you say navy street? The mariner?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mariner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marina.

speaker: interviewee
text: Marina [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- what was I-- I lost my thought. And my school was at number two on the same street. On the big break at 11 o'clock, I ran home to eat something. It was only five, six houses from mine-- from where I lived-- to this-- the school. It was another house on the street. It was a double house on the front of the same building-- lived people in apartments. And the next building in the back was the school. Now in the morning, we all assembled in front in the yard. They played the national anthem. They raised the flag. And then-- then we had radio in the school. Then the-- the radio spoke to the children every morning. They had the-- it-- other-- it came from a central place, not from one school, which was some kind of a speech for the children about the day or-- or little events and things. And then we made about five minutes of exercises. Then one by one we went into the classes. We-- in the school were boys only. And the first and the second grade were mixed-- but not in my school-- in some schools. In our schools, it was all boys. And it was seven grades. And it could've been maybe 500, 600-- not that many, maybe less than that. But some of the grades were two classes, like two first grade, or two second grades, you know. Later on, it became less-- maybe about 500 children, maybe 600. We were 60 children in a class. Discipline was very strong. There was no kind of monkey business in-- in class. And this was school. After school, I went home, and I ate something. And I went to cheder. Cheder was right across. We lived at 9, and the cheder was in-- right across from me. I don't remember it was 8 or 6, the number, the same street. And the-- the cheder was maybe, oh, 50, 60 boys. And it was a lot of fun. A lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of horsing around. It was not such a strong discipline like in public school. And this we paid privately. I don't remember how much, maybe a zlot a week or something like that. A zlot meant the let's say, like $1. But a zlot was very strong. It was-- the currency was very strong. And money was not flowing around in the neighborhood. There was never full employment in the neighborhood. People worked part-time. Some have full-time jobs. But there was never 100% employment. And in the building I lived, I have seen a lot of poor, very poor people, you know. And there wasn't much like support like here. There was the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] or what would you-- we called it the Kehillah, which means like the Jewish agency. They helped some, but they too-- the money didn't come in. You know, it wasn't-- it was not rich. It was poor. I wouldn't say that there weren't rich people, even in the neighborhood. There were. There were rich people in Balut. Some were very wealthy, but the-- the general population was not. The Jewish life was very rich. We had at least, not to exaggerate, at least four or five daily Yiddish newspapers. In our house, we had a-- the-- the-- a Jewish newspaper every day. And on the weekend, the-- the Jewish newspaper for the weekend came out with a supplement like with pictures and things-- just like here, Sunday paper. We also bought the Polish paper. And since I could read Yiddish-- not perfect-- Polish was easier. So naturally, I had the Polish paper. But I have no problem reading Jewish either. I used to-- we used to go-- I used to go to the library and get books. I liked to read a lot. I spent a lot of time reading. Going to the movies, was for mine age, the biggest entertainment. But there was a lot of other entertainments. There were dance halls where the young people get together. There were all kind of organizations were by the dozens, you know. The-- the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] or the [NON-ENGLISH] or-- or the Zionist and-- and the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the biggest party was-- was the Bund. And being in the neighborhood where I lived, most people supported the Bund. The Bund was the socialist party of Poland. But the Jewish section was separate. So they had this big hall, and I used to go along with some of the friends of mine. And we had a lot of fun there. We played games. We learned songs. We sang songs. And it was all right. It took away-- in other words, I was always busy. I always had enough time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I always-- I never had enough time just to fool around. That was fooling around, but it was fun. Now where am I going now? And-- When did that start to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now I'll go a little-- now myself, religiously, on the holidays, I had to go to the temple. There was no way out of it. Every day I had to put on my tefillin, which are the phylacteries, or whatever they're called in English. And I had to say my prayers in the morning. Now in the middle of the week, this is about all I had to do. But on-- on-- on Saturday, my father went to the temple. But not all-- I wouldn't say he went every Saturday. But the holidays, this was a sure thing. The whole family had to go. In the house, everything was separated. There was for-- for the milk-- milk stuff, and meat in separate drawers, separate dishes, separate-- let's say for the-- for the milk we had those spoons which were white covered porcelain. They were metal but with a porcelain cover-- white. And if sometimes a spoon got mixed up with the other, it was a big tragedy for my mother. Of course to us, you know, it wasn't any mortal strict. I did not eat any non-Kosher food till the war. I wouldn't even think about it. I would think I would choke if I would eat something which is not Kosher. And-- now when it came short to the war, I got old enough to realize what's going on politically. And I started to-- I listened. Maybe I didn't start, but I listened what happened around the house, the talks in the house. And they were starting to talk about Germany-- how bad it is. And I remember when it happened in-- in-- in [NON-ENGLISH] there was some kind of a riot. I remember that it was talked in the house. There was-- there was rape. There was killing. And people started getting more conscience politically what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been around '37 or '38. We-- I always knew that Hitler was there, you know. When you went to the movies, which I went at least once, sometimes twice a week. And between they would gave-- they always gave two features. And in between they had the news reel, or news. And we would always see the-- the German army with the-- marching and all that. And we would start whistling, you know, and all that. So we were always conscious. I could hear Hitler on radio. Of course, I didn't understand. But some people did and listened to his speeches. A radio was a luxury, but some people had-- had a radio. A telephone was a luxury.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You know, we're gonna have to change tapes now, OK? But I will remember where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]
